text: The laparoscopic view through the left upper quadrant port reveals loops of small bowel adherent to the bladder and anterior abdominal wall. The surgeon carefully dissects to access the abscess cavity beneath the bladder, avoiding injury to the small bowel. The appendix is visible on the right side of the screen, plastered underneath the bladder. Upon entering the abscess cavity, the surgeon prepares to take cultures and perform suction and irrigation.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection to separate inflammatory adhesions, aiming to unroof the abscess cavity located between the bladder and uterus. The terminal ileum is visible being lifted from the right pelvic side wall, revealing the cavity beneath the bladder and anterior to the uterus.

The surgeon performs irrigation to ensure the absence of debris beneath the uterus and in the deep pelvis near the rectum. The appendix is visibly adherent to the terminal ileum, with inflammatory adhesions present. The surgeon carefully separates these adhesions, focusing on the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum that is stuck to the appendix. The mesoappendix is dissected to facilitate separation.

The surgeon struggles to visualize the base of the appendix due to adhesions with the anti-mesenteric fat of the terminal ileum (TI). The base of the appendix is exposed and divided to improve angles for further dissection. The appendix, adhered to the TI and its fat, is separated using a ligature for finger fracture technique, then placed in a bag for removal.

The surgeon performs a final inspection of the staple line and the abscess cavity, followed by additional irrigation. The cavity is filled with omentum to promote healing and tissue regeneration. No drain is left due to the localized nature of the abscess. The surgical field is clear, and the procedure concludes successfully.
